course_description:
This course provides an opportunity to develop research skills and specialist knowledge through a guided independent research project.

ADPS7060 provides students with the opportunity to undertake a focused research task in a specific area of research interest, conducted through a series of advisory meetings and self-directed study. The course expects students to achieve an advanced level of academic argument and the mastery of instruments for conducting and expressing that argument. The research project will result in a body of work that communicates the process, outcomes and value of the research. The mode of communication and scope shall be agreed upon between the student and the advisor. Students submit a Thesis describing the research and outcomes in a durable and communicable scholarly form. The Thesis may include written analysis, fieldwork reports, computational modelling, surveys, designs and other creative work, material experiments, or any other record and analysis of the program of research agreed with the Advisor. Students commencing in Semester 1 enrol in ADPS7060 for Sem 1 and Sem 2; Students commencing in semester 2 enrol in ADPS7061 for Sem 2 and the following Sem 1.

Information on enrolling in ADPS7060 is available on the School of Architecture, Design and Planning website: https://adp.uq.edu.au/apply-coursework-research-thesis.

ADPS7060 is a guided independent research project conducted through a series of advisory meetings and self-directed study. It provides students with the opportunity to undertake a focussed research task in a specific area of research interest. The course expects students to achieve an advanced level of academic argument and the mastery of instruments for constructing and expressing that argument.

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:
Completion of 8 units of the MArch, MUPlan, or MUrbDevDes and a cumulative GPA of 5.0 or higher
incompatible: You can't enrol in this course if you've already completed the following:
restrictions: MArch, MURP, or MUrbDevDes students only.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
Group meetings and seminars will be organised to bring students together. These activities will alternate with individual meeting with thesis supervisor. Students will be notified about the schedule of these at the start of the semester.
course_aims: This course aims to develop students' capacity to plan and implement an independent research project on a topic relevant to the disciplines of architecture, planning or urban design.

learning_outcomes.description: Critically analyse, evaluate and position an argument on a body of literature or creative works according to disciplinary conventions.
Articulate a relevant research problem and contextualise your research question/s within its field.
Design a coherent methodology including appropriate research method/s that answer the stated research question/s.
Implement the research design through data collection or data-informed design practice.
Generate and synthesise findings through data analysis or reflective design practice.
Select and apply a range of communication strategies, including written, verbal and visual methods, according to disciplinary standards.
Reflect on appropriate, informed and ethical use of a range of research tools and methods, including AI where utilised.

assessment_summary.title: Assessment 1 – Research Plan and Annotated Bibliography
Assessment 2 – Literature Review, Methodology & Ethical Reflections
Assessment 3 – Thesis Development and Final Document

assessment_details.title: Assessment 1 – Research Plan and Annotated Bibliography
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment Rationale: This assessment establishes the intellectual foundation for the thesis. You will clearly articulate a relevant research problem and research question(s), and propose a coherent and feasible research plan. You will critically analyse and annotate relevant literature and/or creative works. Emphasis is placed on disciplinary-appropriate written and visual communication, as well as early ethical awareness, including reflective consideration of research tools and AI. The assessment requires students to position their proposed thesis within an appropriate disciplinary context, to identify a relevant research problem, articulate the research intent, scope, and direction. Students may pursue either: · a research-based thesis , in which findings are generated through data collection and analysis, or · a research-led design thesis , in which evidence informs iterative design exploration and reflective practice. Both approaches must demonstrate scholarly rigour, critical judgement, and clarity of intent. Complex / authentic assessment using AI and/or MT to support learning: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The School of Architecture, Design and Planning uses Blackboard and Turnitin for assessment submission. Turnitin is accessed through the course Blackboard site. Turnitin also checks for plagiarism or instances where the original work of others is not appropriately acknowledged. Uploaded files must contain readable text and not be rasterised. Students are advised to commence assignment uploads with sufficient amount of time (consider possible technical problems with computers, internet speed, etc). After successfully submitting an assignment through Turnitin, a ‘Submission Complete!’ screen will be displayed. It is the student’s responsibility to check assignment preview and confirm successful submission. If the ‘Submission Complete!’ screen isn’t displayed, the student should regard the submission as unsuccessful. Students should download a copy of the digital receipt as proof they have submitted the assignment. Students who are experiencing upload issues must advise the Course Coordinator immediately by email and should include screenshots and a copy of the assessment for submission.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
If a student wishes to apply for an extension, they must apply online on or before the assignment due date. When possible, it is suggested that requests are submitted 2 business days prior to the submission due date for the assignment to allow processing time. Students with outstanding applications for extensions are advised to submit their assessment by the original due date, irrespective of whether the work is complete, so that what has been done can be graded.
Late submission
The late penalty for this assessment item will be calculated as follows:
1st hour block - initial 1 hour grace period no penalty.
2nd hour to 8th hour block - 10% of the maximum possible mark allocated for the assessment item (or 1 grade) will be deducted per hour up to a maximum of 70%
Any submissions received after eight hours will not receive any marks unless an extension has been approved.
Each one-hour block is recorded from the time the submission is due.

assessment_details.title: Assessment 2 – Literature Review, Methodology & Ethical Reflections
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment Rationale: This assessment consolidates and formalises your thesis framework. You will achieve a depth and critical synthesis of literature and/or creative works, the clarity and relevance of the research problem and research question(s), and the coherence, rigour, and justification of the proposed methodology. Strong emphasis is placed on alignment between research intent, method, and anticipated outputs, as well as the quality and appropriateness of written and visual communication. Ethical awareness and reflective use of research tools, including AI, are integral at this stage. The assessment marks the point at which the thesis moves from exploration to commitment. Students must demonstrate that their research problem is clearly articulated, well-positioned within the discipline, and supported by an appropriate and feasible research design. Formative Feedback: You should be meeting and discussing drafts regularly with your advisor(s) during teaching weeks. Formative feedback is in Week 10 of Semester 1 2026, prior to which a draft chapter should be submitted directly to your advisor for feedback. Following the examination of the thesis, students will be required to make any recommended corrections or amendments to the satisfaction of the supervisor and course coordinator, and may choose to upload the corrected thesis to e-space. Complex / authentic assessment using AI and/or MT to support learning: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. assessment_details.title: Assessment 3 – Thesis Development and Final Document
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment Rationale: This assessment requires students to demonstrate the application of their approved research methodology and to present emerging findings from their thesis project. Students are expected to show evidence of substantive research activity, including data collection, analysis, and/or data-informed design exploration. The assessment marks the transition from research design to research execution. Students must demonstrate that their methodological approach has been implemented as proposed, or appropriately adapted in response to findings, and that initial conclusions or insights have emerged. Students may present findings generated through: · data-driven research and analysis , or · research-led design practice , in which evidence informs iterative design testing and reflective evaluation. Both approaches must demonstrate rigour, coherence, and critical engagement with the research problem. Complex / authentic assessment using AI and/or MT to support learning: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 12 (23 Feb - 24 May)
learning_activities.topic: Supervision Regular weekly or fortnightly meetings with your advisor, to the equivalent of one hour per week. Regular attendance and showing work in progress is a requirement.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 12 (23 Feb - 24 May)
learning_activities.topic: Workshops A briefing on course requirements for thesis students. A short description of topics will be presented by students. Please make sure you have met your supervisor in Week 1 and have confirmed your topic. Workshop will be offered to support thesis structuring and writing.

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Learning activities
